coredata.dc:title: A ‘‘Turn-on’’ Al(III)-Mediated Fluorescent Micro-Probe for Quercetin Sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Xiqi
Sun, Wenwen
Wang, Le
Zhang, Xiao
Liu, Mengxia
Qu, Yi
Ming, Li-June
coredata.dc:description: Quercetin (Qr) is a common flavonoid widely found in plants with promising pharmacological activities. It is thus worthwhile to design a convenient method for its detection. In this work, a specific Al3+-regulated poly(cyclotriphosphazene-co-curcumin) (PC-Al) micro-probe was designed for real-time detection of Qr, therein significant “turn-on” fluorescence was observed after addition of Qr to PC-Al. Under optimal conditions, the probe has a detection limit of 23.2 nM for Qr sensing with good linearity in the range of 0–5 × 10−5 M. Moreover, real-time applications of the PC-Al probe in red wine, orange juice, and honey samples were validated. Furthermore, assisted by a consumer smartphone sensing platform, the sensor shows an excellent linear response between the red/blue ratio (R/B) of the two color channels in the picture and the concentration of Qr in the range of 1–120 μM, achieving quantitative detection of Qr. The introduction of smartphone system to realize convenient detection of Qr demonstrates good application prospects of this Al3+-mediated fluorescence probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quercetin
Al3+
Turn-on
Fluorescence Micro-Probe
Smartphone sensing

Preparation of test paper for the detection of Qr
Visual detection of Qr with test strips

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the Al3+-mediated “turn-on” fluorescent micro-probe, cross-linked poly(cyclotriphosphazene-co-curcumin) PC-Al, for Qr sensing monitored with readily available cellphones technology.
(a) Fluorescence intensity variation of probe PC-Al immersed in EtOH for various times. (b) The time-dependent fluorescence of probe PC-Al in EtOH suspension for sensing Qr.

Fluorescence intensity at 500 nm of PC-Al probe with (a) different organic compounds (20 μM) as labeled and the photographs of the probe with these compounds (inset, from left to right: Qr, GSH, Cys, His, Glc, AA, tanin, catechol, daidzein and baicalein), (b) cations (20 μM) as labeled and the photographs of the probe with the cations (inset, from left to right: Qr, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+), and (c) anions (20 μM) as labeled and the photographs of the probe with the anions (inset, from left to right: Qr, Cl−, AcO−, H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−) in ethanol and (d) comparison of fluorescence intensity (at 500 nm) of PC-Al in the presence of various competing species of organic compounds, cations, and anions (all at 200 μM) during Qr sensing (20 μM) in ethanol.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of PC-Al with different Qr concentrations over the range of 0–6.5 × 10−5 M; (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Qr concentration.

Fluorescence color images of the PC-Al test strips at different Qr concentrations (from left to right: 0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10 and 0.12 mM) under a 365 nm UV lamp and the relationship between R/B and Qr concentration with a color-scanning APP.
1H NMR of curcumin (Cur), quercetin (Qr) and mixture of Cur:Al:Qr = 1:1:1 (DMSO-d6).
The results for the determination of Qr in real samples. (n=3).

head.ce:title: A ‘‘Turn-on’’ Al(III)-Mediated Fluorescent Micro-Probe for Quercetin Sensing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of South Florida, Tampa, FL 33620-5250, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of South Florida

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this research equally as the first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quercetin (Qr) is a common flavonoid widely found in plants with promising pharmacological activities. It is thus worthwhile to design a convenient method for its detection. In this work, a specific Al3+-regulated poly(cyclotriphosphazene-co-curcumin) (PC-Al) micro-probe was designed for real-time detection of Qr, therein significant “turn-on” fluorescence was observed after addition of Qr to PC-Al. Under optimal conditions, the probe has a detection limit of 23.2 nM for Qr sensing with good linearity in the range of 0–5 × 10−5 M. Moreover, real-time applications of the PC-Al probe in red wine, orange juice, and honey samples were validated. Furthermore, assisted by a consumer smartphone sensing platform, the sensor shows an excellent linear response between the red/blue ratio (R/B) of the two color channels in the picture and the concentration of Qr in the range of 1–120 μM, achieving quantitative detection of Qr. The introduction of smartphone system to realize convenient detection of Qr demonstrates good application prospects of this Al3+-mediated fluorescence probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin (3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone, Qr), as one of the most studied flavonols in nature, is widely found in a variety of vegetables, fruits, and traditional Chinese herbs [1,2], and has received broad interests in biomedicine fields [3–6]. For instance, quercetin was found effective in treating various diseases and disorders, including chronic bronchitis, high blood pressure, and eye disease caused by diabetes and cataracts [7–13]. Due to the pharmacological and biological activities of Qr, it is essential to establish a simple and sensitive method for its detection [14–18]. At present, several analytical methods such as capillary electrophoresis, UV, HPLC, etc. have been used to detect and track Qr with various sensing systems [19,20]. Fluorescent probes possess desired properties of high sensitivity and real-time imaging, and have been reported for the detection of Qr [21–33], including small molecule fluorescent probes [22], metal-associated nanoparticles [23–26], and micro/nano probes based on carbon quantum dots [27–33]. However, majority of fluorescence-detection methods are based on deminishment of the probe's fluorescence by Qr, which can affect the accuracy of the assay. As the contents of Qr in many plants are very low, typically at the micromolar levels [34], thus it is of significance to design fluorescence-enhancing probes for sensitive Qr sensing.

In this paper, preparation of poly(cyclotriphosphazene-co-curcumin) microspheres (PC) and their use for fluorescence-enhanced detection of Qr are discussed. Cross-coupling of curcumin with cyclotriphosphazene can form PC microspheres ( Fig. 1). Due to the excellent β-ketoketone metal-binding site of curcumin in PC, the Al3+-modified PC microprobe (PC-Al) can be easily formed. Upon addition of Qr, the PC-Al probe shows “turn-on” fluorescence for sensitive Qr sensing. Notably, assisted by a consumer’s smartphone sensing platform, the PC-Al sensor exhibited an excellent linear response between the red/blue (R/B) ratio and the concentration of Qr (1–120 μM), achieving quantitative Qr sensing (Fig. 1).

Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals and solvents were commercially available and used directly without further purification. Hexachlorocyclotriphosphazene, curcumin (Cur), Qr, triethylamine, ethanol, glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), glucose (Glc), ascorbic acid (AA), tannin, catechol, daidzein, baicalein were purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). And all the metal salts were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was purified from Millipore equipment.

The cyclotriphosphazene-based fluorescent microspheres (PC) were synthesized according to the literature, and the optimal conditions were improved (See supplementary information). The probe PC-Al was prepared by properly mixing certain amounts of 12 mg/mL PC methanol suspension and 100 mg/mL Al(NO3)3•9 H2O aqueous solution, and then diluted with ethanol to produce final concentrations of 1 mg/mL and 1.2 mg/mL for PC and Al3+, respectively.

First, the PC microprobes above and a series of guest solutions, including catechol, daidzein, and baicalein ethanol solutions, GSH, Cys, His, Glc, AA, tannin, and metal ion aqueous solutions were prepared separately. Then fluorescence experiments were performed by adding 10 μL of the PC suspension, 20 μL of the Al(NO3)3•9 H2O solution, and various volumes of the Qr stock solution (2 mM) or a guest solution in a cuvette with 2 mL of ethanol and mixing thoroughly, and then excited at 372 nm and the emission spectra recorded in the range of 400–800 nm. The suspensions were kept uniform with ultrasound, and each solution was measured 3 times to reduce bias.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Qr in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Wine, orange juice, and honey water were purchased from local supermarkets (Shanghai, China). First, a certain amount of a sample was centrifuged and the supernatant filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, and an amount of 20 μL aliquot diluted into 2 mL ethanol for detection. The detection procedures were the same as in Section 2.3. All the filtered samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of test paper for the detection of Qr
ce:section.ce:para: The ordinary laboratory filter paper was cut into appropriate rectangular strips to make the test strips, which was then immersed into 5 mL ethanol suspension of PC-Al probe for 10 min and dried at room temperature. For sensing measurements, the prepared test strips were immersed in different concentrations of Qr solution (0–0.12 mM) for 1 min, then dried in the air. The fluorescence changes of the test strips were then visualized under a 365-nm UV lamp. The R/B light intensity ratio of each sample was then analyzed with a cellphone.

The PC spheres can form complexes with different metal ions and are thus used for screening various metal ions as suitable fluorescent probes for detecting Qr which is known to bind metal ions [22–26,35]. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Materials), among the 15 diverse metal ions screened, including Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Eu3+, Tb3+, and Yb3+, the PC-Al probe prepared by Al3+ ions had the most pronounced fluorescence response to Q even though Co2+ and Yb3+ that show weak fluorescence output have been verified to bind to Qr by means of paramagnetic NMR [35]. Thus, Al3+ is chosen as a suitable metal ion to mediate the PC probe for further investigation of Qr detection. In the presence of Qr, the probe PC-Al shows bright green fluorescence with a maximum emission at 500 nm. To optimize the conditions for the detection of Qr, the solvent system and the probe concentration were varied. Several different polar solvents were screened, wherein ethanol showed the best response (Fig. S2). Then, titration experiments were conducted, showing that a mass ratio of PC:Al3+ = 1:1.2 reached saturation (Fig. S3). The change in PC absorption spectrum after the addition of Al3+ indicated formation of the PC-Al probe. Addition of Qr further changed the absorption spectrum of PC-Al, indicating formation of a ternary complex PC-Al-Qr (Fig. S4).

The stability of the probe PC-Al in ethanol for Qr sensing was further studied by immersing the probe in an ethanol solution for 2 h at room temperature, showing little change of the fluorescence which indicates high stability of the probe in ethanol ( Fig. 2 a). The reaction time of the probe for sensing Qr was also tested and exhibited a rapid response to Qr within 1 min and nearly 40-fold fluorescence enhancement within 2 min (Fig. 2 b). As the results presented, the probe can be used for real-time monitoring of Qr. The probe exhibits excellent stability and rapid response to meet the detection conditions in daily life and could be a valuable candidate for practical use.

To investigate the selectivity of probe PC-Al, various possible interferent compounds and ions were tested which includes catechol, daidzein, baicalein, glutathione, Cys, His, glucose (Glc), ascorbic acid (AA), and tannin ( Fig. 3 a), cations Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Mg2+ (Fig. 3 b), and anions Cl−, ACO−, H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, and SO4 2− (Fig. 3 c). As shown in Fig. 3 insets, bright green fluorescence is observed only in the presence of Qr under a 365-nm UV lamp, and the addition of the interferent molecules/ions exhibited no apparent change of the fluorescence under the same conditions. The observations indicate that this probe indeed exhibits superior selectivity toward Qr.

Since detectability of the probe might be susceptible to interference, potential interferents must be tested for evaluating selectivity of the probes. Therefore, whether or not the sensing behavior of the PC-Al probe toward Qr can be affected by interfering substances should be evaluated. Thus, a 10-fold excess amount (200 μM) of an interfering substance was added to the PC-Al probe system prior to sensing of 20-μM Qr to reveal any interference. As shown in Fig. 3 d, the presence of the compounds with structural motives analogous to Qr (e.g., catechol, daidzein, and baicalein) and some other potential interfering compounds such as those found in red wine, orange juice, and honey (cf. Section 3.3) do not interfere with Qr sensing. Moreover, some other metal ions and anions also do not interfere with the detection of Qr (Fig. 3 d). The results indicates that the probe has excellent anti-interference properties and is expected to be a promising candidate for Qr detection in complex systems (cf. Section 3.3).

A well-determined limit of detection (LOD) is one of the criteria for the applicability of sensors. The LOD is calculated from the fluorescence titration data based on the equation LOD = 3δ/k [36], wherein δ is the standardized deviation of the assay values (of the 11 samples herein) and k denotes the slope of the linear equation. Accordingly, the LOD of the probe PC-Al is obtained as 23.2 nM for Qr detection ( Fig. 4) with a linear range of (0−5) × 10−5 M, which is superior to many reported results (Table S1).

To demonstrate potential applications of the PC-Al probe, quantitative detection of Qr in red wine, orange juice, and honey was verified by the standard addition method. Therein, Qr was added to the samples to render final concentrations of 5, 10, and 15 μM, and the fluorescence recorded. Since the preparation of the samples for detection under the established optimal conditions requires dilution of the sample into ethanol, which renders the Qr level very low as the concentrations of Qr in some red wines vary significantly in a range of 0.16–16.21 mg/L [37] (0.53–53.6 μM), orange juice in a range of 9.0–13.4 mg/kg [38] [(29.8–44.3)/d μM, d = density], and 3.6 mg/kg in honey [39] (11.9/d μM). The deviations of the measurements from the standard addition of Qr were determined to be 0.19, 0.32, and 0.35 μM ( Table 1) which are within the bulk range of Qr in red wine, orange juice, and honey [37–39] after the test samples were calibrated by the 100-fold dilution for measurement under the experimental conditions herein. The recovery (97.76–104.99 %) and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.22–3.42 % are within the reasonable range (Table 1), which indicates our proposed sensing method by the use of the PC-Al probe in real sample is quite suitable. In conclusion, this Al3+-specific cross-linked poly(cyclictriphosphozene-co-curcumin) fluorescent probe PC-Al exhibits significant potential for sensing Qr in real samples which warrants further explorations to establish quantification protocols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Qr with test strips
ce:section.ce:para:
For simple and efficient detection of Qr in real samples, facile and economical test paper strips are designed. When the test trips are immersed in solutions of different Qr concentrations, their fluorescence immediately changes from dark blue to bright green under a 365 nm UV lamp ( Fig. 5). To realize the potential of the test strips for quantifying Qr by the use of a universal device, we use a smartphone installed with a color scanner APP to achieve fluorescence digitization [40–42]. The RGB values of red (R) and blue (B) in the detected images are extracted through the color recognition APP, which allows correlation of Qr concentrations and the RGB values to be established. As the concentration of Qr increases, the color of the test strips gradually changes, suggesting that the red/blue (R/B) ratio of each image may be closely associated with the concentration of Qr. A linear relationship can be established between the R/B ratio and Qr concentration in the range of 0–0.12 mM, showing the linear correlation below,
R/B = 4.961 [Qr] + 0.082
with a correlation coefficient of 0.993 (Fig. 5). The detection limit of the test strips for sensing Qr is determined to be 6.39 μM calculated according to the equation LOD = 3δ/k [36]. Although this value is higher than that determined on the basis of fluorescence intensity, the results demonstrate that a low-cost Qr assay, which does not require special reaction media and complex preparations, is suitable and easily accessible for visualization of Qr quantification. Moreover, this LOD by the use of the convenient smartphone technology is within the range of Qr concentrations in real samples [37–39] which demonstrates potential applications of this method for Qr sensing in a wide range of samples. The use of test trips and quantified by the use of a smartphone is a much more convenient and readily available way to quantify Qr, which can be expected to extend to sense other molecules of great interest.

Quercetin is a polyhydroxy-substituted aromatic compound that may act as a Lewis base and form complexes with metal ions [22–26,35]. We speculate that the Qr-sensing mechanism by the PC-Al probe is due to Qr interaction with the metal center and formation of the ternary complex PC-Al-Qr, which results in fluorescence enhancement due to the complexation of Al3+ with Qr [43,44]. To gain further insights into the detection mechanism, the metal-binding curcumin moiety of PC spheres and its potential interaction with Qr was investigated by means of NMR and mass spectrometry. The disappearance of the characteristic 1H NMR peaks of β-diketone (or its keto-enol tautomer) in curcumin (16.46 ppm) and 5-OH in quercetin (12.49 ppm) upon mixing 1:1:1 of curcumin, Al3+, and Qr ( Fig. 6) suggests potential interaction among curcumin, Al3+, and Qr to form a Cur-Al-Qr ternary complex which causes the fluorescence change. The sharpness and significant downfield shift of the 5-OH signal can be attributed to the internal H-bonding between the 5-OH proton and the β-keto group while its disappearance upon metal binding can be attributed to significant disturbance of the H-bond and/or deprotonation due to metal binding. This 4,5-β-ketophenolate Al3+-binding mode is different from the binding of transition metal and lanthanide ions (Co2+ and Yb3 +) to Qr at the 3,4-α-ketoenolate site [35], which may be a reason that the specific Al3+-mediated sensing is not much affected by the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 3). Furthermore, the possible ternary complex is confirmed with mass spectrometry by showing the parent ion of the complex [Cur-Al-Qr]+ with m/z of 695.1340 (calc’d. 695.1340) (Fig. S5). These results demonstrate that the formation of the PC-Al-Qr ternary system between PC-Al and Qr is the main reason for achieving fluorescence-enhanced sensing.

In conclusion, we report herein a special Al3+-mediated poly(cyclotriphosphazene-co-curcumin) micro-probe (PC-Al) for visual detection of Qr. This probe exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Qr with a very sensitive LOD value of 23.2 nM and a fast sensing time of 1 min. In addition, the detection of Qr in food products (i.e., red wine, orange juice, and honey) by the use of this probe was verified. The selectivity and response time for sensing Qr are not interfered by more than two-dozen molecules and ions. The sensing mechanism attributed to the formation of a ternary moiety of curcumin-Al-Qr has also been postulated and validated by means of NMR and mass spectrometry.

Inexpensive test strips with adsorbed PC-Al are demonstrated to be convenient and effective sensing device for real-time detection of Qr. Moreover, quantitative analysis of Qr sensing by the test strips has been achieved from the ratio of the two color channels (R/B) obtained with a color scan app on a smartphone, showing a linear correlation with Qr concentration in the range of 1–120 μM. Taken together, this new method has the advantage of being efficient and reliable that does not require sophisticated instrumentation, making it valuable for Qr sensing and also providing a new idea for visual detection of Qr and other biologically and environmentally significant molecules.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors.

Xiqi Su: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenwen Sun: Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Le Wang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiao Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Mengxia Liu: Writing - review & editing. Yi Qu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Li-June Ming: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the SUES Distinguished Overseas Professorship Program (2017-2023), Shanghai, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136497.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Promising bioactive properties of quercetin for potential food applications and health benefits: a review

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiqi Su received her master's degree from Shanghai University of Engineering Science in 2019, and her research focuses on metal-based complex probes for the detection of bioactive small molecules.

Wenwen Sun received her bachelor's degree from Yancheng institute of technology (Jiangsu, China) in 2022. She is currently pursuing her master's degree at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science. Currently, her main research interests are in fluorescent probing of bioactive small molecules.

Le Wang obtained her PhD degree in 2010 from Zhengzhou University, at which she works on phosphorus chemistry. In 2011, she joined the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science as a teacher. Her current research interests are focused on the design, synthesis and application of phosphorus-containing compounds.

Xiao Zhang obtained her bachelor degree in 2021 in Henan Normal University (Henan, China). She is at the moment pursuing her master degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in SUES. Her current researches focus on the design and synthesis of probe and DFT calculation.

Mengxia Liu is currently pursuing her master degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science (SUES), with a primary focus on the synthesis and analysis of PPI sensors.

Yi Qu received his PhD degree from East China University of Science and Technology in 2012 for his research on organic fluorescent functional materials. He joined the faculty of the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering and Technology in 2014. His current research focuses on the rapid detection of organic fluorescent probes for food, environmental and biological samples.

Li-June Ming graduated from the University of California, Los Angeles, and is now on the faculty at the University of South Florida, where he is engaged in the study of inorganic metallodrugs and their biological properties, with research focusing on: nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), as well as kinetic.
